results.question: How does the RingCentral chatbot framework (ringcentral-chatbot-js) relate to the core JS SDK (@ringcentral/sdk)?
results.ground_truth: ringcentral-chatbot-js is a separate higher-level framework built on top of the RingCentral platform. It handles bot-specific concerns: webhook routing, skill system, conversation state management. Under the hood it uses the same platform APIs as @ringcentral/sdk for authentication and API calls, but abstracts them behind a bot-friendly interface.

raw.answer:
`ringcentral-chatbot-js` and `@ringcentral/sdk` sit at different layers. Both talk to the same RingCentral Platform REST APIs, but they target different use cases.

## Layering

| | `@ringcentral/sdk` | `ringcentral-chatbot-js` |
|---|---|---|
| **Role** | Official general-purpose JS SDK (auth, SMS, call logs, subscriptions, etc.) | Higher-level Team Messaging bot framework |
| **npm package** | `@ringcentral/sdk` | `ringcentral-chatbot` |
| **Monorepo path** | `sdks/ringcentral-js` | `chatbots/ringcentral-chatbot-js` |

## Same platform, not a direct SDK wrapper

The Team Messaging docs describe the relationship this way:

> All three use the same platform APIs as `@ringcentral/sdk` underneath but abstract the bot-specific boilerplate. They are separate frameworks built on the RingCentral platform — **not direct wrappers of the SDK** — but they share the same underlying API surface.

So the relationship is **architectural**, not a strict npm dependency chain. In practice, `ringcentral-chatbot-js` does **not** list `@ringcentral/sdk` in its dependencies — it uses `@rc-ex/core` (from `sdks/ringcentral-extensible`) for auth and API calls:

```1:6:chatbots/ringcentral-chatbot-js/src/models/Bot.ts
import Sequelize from 'sequelize';
import RingCentral from '@rc-ex/core';
import waitFor from 'wait-for-async';
import FormData from 'form-data';
import TokenInfo from '@rc-ex/core/lib/definitions/TokenInfo';
import RestException from '@rc-ex/core/lib/RestException';
```

Both `@ringcentral/sdk` and `@rc-ex/core` are alternative JS clients for the same REST platform.

## What the chatbot framework adds

Where `@ringcentral/sdk` gives you low-level API access, `ringcentral-chatbot-js` adds bot-specific infrastructure so you can focus on business logic:

- **Webhook routing** and event handling (`Message4Bot`, group join/leave, etc.)
- **Skill system** for modular bot capabilities
- **Conversation state management** (database persistence via Sequelize)
- **Bot token and webhook lifecycle** (OAuth, subscription setup, maintenance)
- **Express or AWS Lambda deployment** scaffolding

The framework’s stated philosophy is to let developers focus on bot logic instead of RingCentral/Glip platform implementation details.

## When to use which

- **`@ringcentral/sdk`** — general RingCentral integrations (voice, SMS, analytics, etc.)
- **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** — Team Messaging bots; RingCentral recommends it as the starting point for bot development over wiring everything manually with the SDK

Related frameworks in the same ecosystem: `ringcentral-chatbot-python` (Python equivalent) and `ringcentral-chatbot-core` (shared core abstractions extracted from the JS framework).

mintlify.answer:
## Relationship overview

In RingCentral’s docs, **`@ringcentral/sdk`** and **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** sit at different layers:

| Package | Role |
|---|---|
| **`@ringcentral/sdk`** | Official JavaScript/Node.js REST API SDK for RingCentral platform APIs |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** | Higher-level Team Messaging **bot framework** (skill routing, state, webhooks, Express server) |

The docs state explicitly:

> **All three** [official chatbot frameworks] **use the same platform APIs as `@ringcentral/sdk` underneath** but abstract the bot-specific boilerplate. **They are separate frameworks built on the RingCentral platform — not direct wrappers of the SDK** — but they share the same underlying API surface.

Source: [Team Messaging index — Build a bot](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/team-messaging/index#build-a-bot)

So they are **not** the same library, and the chatbot framework is **not** a thin wrapper around `@ringcentral/sdk`. Both talk to the same REST API surface; the chatbot framework adds bot-specific structure on top.

---

## What each package is for

### `@ringcentral/sdk` — core SDK

- Official JS/Node REST client for RingCentral APIs
- Install:

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/sdk
```

- Used directly for general API work (auth, REST calls, subscriptions), e.g. Team Messaging quick starts:

```bash
npm install @ringcentral/sdk dotenv --save
```

Source: [SDKs](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/sdks#ringcentral-ringex-sdks), [Team Messaging quick start](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/team-messaging/quick-start#create-app-and-obtain-credentials)

### `ringcentral-chatbot-js` — bot framework

- Recommended starting point for **Team Messaging bots**
- Provides:
  - Skill routing
  - State management
  - Webhook handling
  - A full server stack (Express-based)
- Docs: [RingCentral Bot Framework for JavaScript](https://ringcentral.github.io/ringcentral-chatbot-js/)

Example from the docs:

```js
const createApp = require('ringcentral-chatbot/dist/apps').default

const handle = async event => {
  const { type, text } = event
  if (type === 'Message4Bot' && text === 'ping') {
    // respond to ping
  }
}

const app = createApp(handle)
app.listen(process.env.RINGCENTRAL_CHATBOT_EXPRESS_PORT)
```

Source: [Team Messaging index — Build a bot](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/team-messaging/index#build-a-bot)

---

## When to use which

**Use `ringcentral-chatbot-js`** when you want a conversational Team Messaging bot with less boilerplate. The [bot walkthrough](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/team-messaging/bots/walkthrough) points to it as the faster path:

> Try the [RingCentral bot framework](https://ringcentral.github.io/ringcentral-chatbot-js/) to get up and running faster and with less code.

**Use `@ringcentral/sdk` directly** when you want full control or are not building a bot — e.g. creating teams, posting messages, or wiring OAuth/webhooks yourself. The walkthrough’s [sample bot app](https://github.com/ringcentral-tutorials/ringcentral-bot-nodejs-demo) shows that lower-level approach; the docs then recommend the JS bot framework for a more complete bot server out of the box.

---

## Related packages in the same family

RingCentral documents three official chatbot frameworks that share this same relationship to `@ringcentral/sdk`:

| Framework | Language | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** | JavaScript/Node.js | Full framework — **recommended for getting started** |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-python`** | Python | Auth, subscriptions, persistence, AWS Lambda hosting |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-core`** | TypeScript | Core abstractions (events, messaging, Adaptive Cards) without the full server stack |

All three use the same platform APIs as `@ringcentral/sdk` underneath, per the docs.

---

## Bottom line

- **`@ringcentral/sdk`** = general-purpose REST SDK for Node.js  
- **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** = bot-specific framework on the same API surface, with bot plumbing built in  
- They are **complementary**, not interchangeable: same underlying APIs, different abstraction level — the chatbot framework is **not** a direct SDK wrapper, but it targets the same platform endpoints the SDK would call.

scores.raw_reasoning: Answer A correctly identifies ringcentral-chatbot-js as a higher-level bot framework that uses the same platform APIs as @ringcentral/sdk while adding webhook routing, skills, and conversation state management, with no significant factual errors.
scores.mintlify_reasoning: Answer B accurately explains the layered relationship, covers all key facts including skill routing, webhooks, state management, and same underlying platform APIs, and correctly notes the chatbot framework is not a direct SDK wrapper.

results.question: What chatbot frameworks does RingCentral provide for building Team Messaging bots? List all available frameworks, their language, and primary purpose.
results.ground_truth: RingCentral provides three official chatbot frameworks: (1) ringcentral-chatbot-js — JavaScript/Node.js, higher-level wrapper with skill routing, state management, and webhook handling; (2) ringcentral-chatbot-python — Python equivalent; (3) ringcentral-chatbot-core — shared core abstractions used by both. All use the same platform APIs as @ringcentral/sdk underneath but remove bot-specific boilerplate. The JS framework is recommended for getting started.

raw.answer:
RingCentral provides **three official chatbot frameworks** for Team Messaging bots. The other repos under `chatbots/` (`rc-assistant`, `ringcentral-conv-ai-demo`) are sample/demo bots, not frameworks.

| Framework | Language | Primary purpose |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** | JavaScript/Node.js (TypeScript) | Full-featured bot server with skill routing, state management, webhook handling, token management, and AWS Lambda deployment — **recommended starting point** |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-python`** | Python | Python equivalent that handles auth tokens, event subscriptions, data persistence (file DB / DynamoDB), and AWS Lambda hosting so developers focus on bot logic in `bot.py` |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-core`** | TypeScript | Standalone core module extracted from the JS framework — bot logic (events, messaging, Adaptive Cards) without the full Express/server stack, for add-ins or other projects that need bot capabilities embedded |

All three sit on the same RingCentral platform APIs as `@ringcentral/sdk` and abstract bot-specific boilerplate (OAuth, webhooks, subscriptions, database setup/maintenance).

**Not frameworks** (for context):
- **`rc-assistant`** — sample Glip bot integrating with AWS Lex and RingCentral data
- **`ringcentral-conv-ai-demo`** — speech-to-text / AI demo app, not a Team Messaging bot framework

The docs also mention community adapters (Botkit, Errbot, Hubot, Lita, Botman), but those are third-party integrations, not official RingCentral frameworks.

mintlify.answer:
RingCentral documents **three official chatbot frameworks** for Team Messaging bots, plus **five third-party framework adapters** on its [Bot Frameworks](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/team-messaging/manual/frameworks) page.

---

## Official RingCentral Frameworks

Per the [Team Messaging overview](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/team-messaging/index#build-a-bot), RingCentral provides **three official chatbot frameworks**:

| Framework | Language | Primary Purpose |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** | JavaScript/Node.js | Full-featured framework with skill routing, state management, and webhook handling — **recommended for getting started** |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-python`** | Python | Python equivalent — handles auth tokens, event subscriptions, persistence, and AWS Lambda hosting |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-core`** | TypeScript | Standalone core abstractions (events, messaging, Adaptive Cards) for projects that need bot capabilities without the full server stack |

All three use the same platform APIs as `@ringcentral/sdk` underneath, but abstract bot-specific boilerplate. They are separate frameworks built on the RingCentral platform — not direct wrappers of the SDK.

### Package & install details

| Framework | Package / docs | Install / usage |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`** | npm package: `ringcentral-chatbot` | Docs: [ringcentral.github.io/ringcentral-chatbot-js](https://ringcentral.github.io/ringcentral-chatbot-js/) |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-python`** | GitHub project: `ringcentral-chatbot-python` | Python framework for Team Messaging bots |
| **`ringcentral-chatbot-core`** | GitHub project: `ringcentral-chatbot-core` | TypeScript core library for custom bot implementations |

**Getting started with `ringcentral-chatbot-js`:**

```js
const createApp = require('ringcentral-chatbot/dist/apps').default

const handle = async event => {
  const { type, text } = event
  if (type === 'Message4Bot' && text === 'ping') {
    // respond to ping
  }
}
const app = createApp(handle)
app.listen(process.env.RINGCENTRAL_CHATBOT_EXPRESS_PORT)
```

The JS framework listens on the port set in **`RINGCENTRAL_CHATBOT_EXPRESS_PORT`**. Under the hood it relies on the same APIs as **`@ringcentral/sdk`** (`npm install @ringcentral/sdk`).

---

## Other Bot Framework Adapters (Team Messaging)

The [Frameworks page](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/team-messaging/manual/frameworks#other-bot-frameworks) also lists **community and RingCentral-maintained adapters** that connect popular third-party bot frameworks to Team Messaging (formerly "Glip"):

| Adapter | Underlying Framework | Language | Primary Purpose |
|---------|---------------------|----------|-----------------|
| **[Botkit Adapter](https://github.com/pkvenu/botkit)** | Botkit | JavaScript/Node.js | Connect Botkit bots to RingCentral Team Messaging |
| **[Errbot Adapter](https://github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-glip-errbot)** | Errbot | Python | Connect Errbot plugins to Team Messaging |
| **[Hubot Adapter](https://github.com/tylerlong/hubot-glip)** | Hubot | CoffeeScript/Node.js | Connect Hubot scripts to Team Messaging |
| **[Lita Adapter](https://github.com/ringcentral-ruby/lita-glip)** | Lita | Ruby | Connect Lita handlers to Team Messaging |
| **[Botman Adapter](https://github.com/ringcentral/glip-botman)** | Botman | PHP | Connect Botman bots to Team Messaging |

These are adapters for existing bot platforms, not standalone RingCentral frameworks. Several are maintained by RingCentral or the `ringcentral-ruby` community (Errbot, Lita, Botman).

---

## Summary

| Category | Count | Frameworks |
|----------|-------|------------|
| **Official RingCentral frameworks** | 3 | `ringcentral-chatbot-js`, `ringcentral-chatbot-python`, `ringcentral-chatbot-core` |
| **Third-party framework adapters** | 5 | Botkit, Errbot, Hubot, Lita, Botman adapters |

For new Team Messaging bots, RingCentral recommends starting with **`ringcentral-chatbot-js`**. Use **`ringcentral-chatbot-python`** for Python/AWS Lambda deployments, or **`ringcentral-chatbot-core`** when you want TypeScript abstractions without the full Express server stack. Use the third-party adapters if you already build on Botkit, Errbot, Hubot, Lita, or Botman.

scores.raw_reasoning: Correctly lists all three official frameworks with accurate languages, purposes (skill routing, state management, webhook handling), @ringcentral/sdk relationship, boilerplate abstraction, and JS as the recommended starting point.
scores.mintlify_reasoning: Correctly and completely covers all three official frameworks with languages, primary purposes, @ringcentral/sdk usage, boilerplate abstraction, and the JS recommendation, with only minor extra context that does not introduce significant errors.

results.question: How do I create a RingCentral Webinar using the API? What key settings are available, and how do I add registrants for a marketing webinar?
results.ground_truth: Create a webinar via the REST API specifying settings including: registrationEnabled (true for marketing webinars — gives each attendee a unique join URL), recordingEnabled, autoRecord, qnaEnabled, pollsEnabled, password, attendeeAuthentication. After creating the webinar, create a session. To add registrants for a marketing webinar, call the create registrant endpoint. Requires EditWebinars and ReadWebinars permissions. Set registrationEnabled=false for internal all-hands webinars (single shared join URL).

raw.answer:
Creating a RingCentral Webinar via the API is a **two-step process**: first create the webinar container, then create a scheduled session. For a marketing webinar, enable registration so each attendee gets a unique join URL.

## Prerequisites

1. **RingCentral Webinar license** (or apply to the [Webinar Partner Developer Program](https://forms.gle/dMRNtnuFnonLQgsu7) for a free developer license).
2. **REST API app** in the [Developer Console](https://developers.ringcentral.com/) with:
   - **Permissions:** `EditWebinars`, `ReadWebinars`
   - **Auth:** JWT (typical for server-side integrations)

---

## Step 1: Create the webinar

**Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars`  
**Permission:** `EditWebinars`

```http
POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "title": "Product Launch Q3 2026",
  "description": "Quarterly product update webinar",
  "settings": {
    "recordingEnabled": true,
    "autoRecord": false,
    "registrationEnabled": true,
    "panelistWaitingRoom": true,
    "attendeeAuthentication": "Guest",
    "password": "s3cur3pass",
    "qnaEnabled": true,
    "pollsEnabled": true,
    "postWebinarRedirectUri": "https://acme.com/thank-you"
  }
}
```

With the Node.js SDK:

```ts
const result = await rc.webinar().configuration().v1().webinars().post({
  title: "Product Launch Q3 2026",
  description: "Quarterly product update webinar",
  settings: { registrationEnabled: true, /* ... */ }
});
```

---

## Step 2: Create a session

A **webinar** holds settings; a **session** is the scheduled occurrence (date/time) that hosts, panelists, and registrants attach to.

**Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions`

```http
POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "scheduledStartTime": "2026-07-15T14:00:00",
  "scheduledDuration": 3600,
  "timeZone": "America/New_York",
  "localeCode": "en-US"
}
```

Required session fields: `scheduledStartTime`, `scheduledDuration` (30–43200 seconds), `timeZone`, and `localeCode`.

---

## Key webinar settings

| Setting | Description |
|---|---|
| `registrationEnabled` | **`true`** for marketing webinars (unique join URL per attendee). **`false`** for internal all-hands (single shared join URL). |
| `recordingEnabled` | Enables recording; must be `true` for other recording settings to apply. |
| `autoRecord` | Starts recording automatically when the webinar goes live. |
| `panelistWaitingRoom` | Places panelists in a waiting room after joining. |
| `attendeeAuthentication` | `Guest`, `AuthenticatedUser`, or `AuthenticatedCoworker`. |
| `password` | Password attendees enter to access the webinar. |
| `qnaEnabled` | Enables Q&A. |
| `pollsEnabled` | Enables polls. |
| `postWebinarRedirectUri` | URL to redirect attendees when the webinar ends. |

For the full list, see the [Webinar API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar).

### Marketing vs. internal webinars

- **Marketing/external (`registrationEnabled: true`):** Each attendee gets a unique join URL for tracking and analytics. Use the registrant API to manage attendees.
- **Internal/all-hands (`registrationEnabled: false`):** One shared join URL; attendees enter their name on join but skip registration questions.

---

## Adding registrants (marketing webinars)

Registration and the registrant API are intended for **marketing webinars** with external attendees. Call the [create registrant](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Registrants/rcwRegCreateRegistrant) operation:

**Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants`

```http
POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "firstName": "Jane",
  "lastName": "Smith",
  "email": "jane@example.com",
  "externalId": "lid=123&oid=456",
  "source": "customer-invite"
}
```

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `firstName`, `lastName`, `email` | Required registrant info |
| `externalId` | Correlate to a CRM contact, lead, or opportunity (e.g. `"lid=123&oid=456"`) |
| `source` | Campaign attribution (e.g. `"customer-invite"`, `"search-ad"`) |
| `joinUri`, `cancellationUri` | Optional custom join/cancel pages on your site |

The response includes a unique `joinUri` for that registrant. You can also build a custom registration page: collect sign-ups on your site, then call this API from backend code.

### Registration preferences (per session)

Manage session-level registration via `PATCH /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}`:

- `registrationStatus` — turn registration on/off
- `autoCloseLimit` — close registration after N registrants
- `suppressEmails` — prevent RingCentral from sending registration emails
- `preventMultipleDeviceJoins` — block joining from multiple devices

### Tracking attendance

Each registrant gets a unique `id`. When they join, they receive a `participantId`. If both are present, they attended; if `participantId` is missing, they never joined. You can also subscribe to registrant webhooks via `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/registrants`.

---

## Summary workflow

```
1. POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars          → create webinar (settings.registrationEnabled: true)
2. POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{id}/sessions  → schedule session
3. POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants  → add each attendee
```

**Note:** Java and C# SDKs do not currently support Webinar APIs; use the REST API directly or the Node.js, Python, PHP, or Ruby SDKs. See the [Webinar Quick Start](docs/webinar/quick-start.mdx) for setup details.

mintlify.answer:
Here is how to create a RingCentral Webinar via the API, configure it for a marketing use case, and add registrants. ## Overview The RingCentral Webinar API is a **two-step process**: 1. **Create the webinar** (title, description, settings) 2. **Schedule a session** (date/time for that webinar) For **marketing webinars**, enable registration so each attendee gets a **unique join URL**. Then add attendees with the **Create Registrant** API. Docs: [Creating webinars](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/webinar/creation), [Quick start](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/webinar/quick-start), [Registrants](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/webinar/registrants) --- ## 1. Prerequisites ### Create a REST API app In the [RingCentral Developer Console](https://developers.ringcentral.com): 1. Create a **REST API App** 2. Auth: **JWT auth flow** 3. Security: private app (callable only with credentials from the same account) Set these environment variables (from the quick start): | Variable | Purpose | |---|---| | `RC_APP_CLIENT_ID` | App Client ID | | `RC_APP_CLIENT_SECRET` | App Client Secret | | `RC_SERVER_URL` | API server (e.g. `https://platform.ringcentral.com`) | | `RC_USER_JWT` | JWT credential for the user | ### Install an SDK | Language | Install command | |---|---| | **JavaScript** | `npm install @ringcentral/sdk dotenv` | | **Python** | `pip3 install ringcentral` | | **PHP** | `curl -sS https://getcomposer.org/installer \| php && php composer.phar require ringcentral/ringcentral-php` | | **Ruby** | `gem install ringcentral-sdk` | Java and C# SDKs do **not** currently support Webinar APIs; call the REST endpoints directly instead. --- ## 2. Create a webinar **Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars` **API Reference:** [Webinar API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar) **Operation:** `rcwConfCreateWebinar` (Create Webinar) **Minimum request body:** ```json { "title": "My first webinar", "description": "This webinar was created via the Webinar Quick Start guide for developers" } ``` **JavaScript example** (from the quick start): ```javascript platform.post('/webinar/configuration/v1/webinars', { title: "My first webinar", description: "This webinar was created via the Webinar Quick Start guide for developers" }) ``` Save the returned **`webinarId`** — you need it to schedule a session. --- ## 3. Schedule a webinar session **Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions` **Example body:** ```json { "scheduledStartTime": "2023-05-10T09:00:00.000Z", "scheduledDuration": 7200, "timezone": "America/New_York" } ``` - `scheduledDuration` is in **seconds** (7200 = 2 hours) - Save the returned **`sessionId`** — you need it to add registrants --- ## 4. Key webinar settings Pass settings when creating (or updating) the webinar under a `settings` object. Common settings from the docs: | Setting | Description | |---|---| | `recordingEnabled` | Enables recording. If `false`, other recording settings cannot be set. | | `autoRecord` | Starts recording automatically when the webinar goes live. | | `panelistWaitingRoom` | Whether panelists go to a waiting room after joining. | | `attendeeAuthentication` | Attendee auth requirement: `Guest`, `AuthenticatedUser`, or `AuthenticatedCoworker` | | `password` | Password attendees must enter to join. | | `qnaEnabled` | Turns Q&A on/off. | | `pollsEnabled` | Turns polls on/off. | | `registrationEnabled` | **Critical for marketing webinars.** Set to `true` for external/marketing webinars; `false` for internal all-hands. | | `postWebinarRedirectUri` | URL to redirect attendees after the webinar ends. | **Marketing webinar example:** ```json { "title": "Product Launch Webinar", "description": "Quarterly customer communication", "settings": { "registrationEnabled": true, "recordingEnabled": true, "autoRecord": true, "qnaEnabled": true, "pollsEnabled": true, "postWebinarRedirectUri": "https://www.acme.com/thankyou" } } ``` ### Why `registrationEnabled` matters | Setting | Behavior | |---|---| | `registrationEnabled: false` | One shared join URL for all attendees (good for internal all-hands). | | `registrationEnabled: true` | Each attendee gets a **unique join URL** for tracking and analytics (good for marketing webinars). | The full list of settings is in the [RingCentral API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar). --- ## 5. Add registrants for a marketing webinar Registrant APIs are intended for **marketing webinars** (external attendees), not internal all-hands meetings. ### Create a registrant **Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants` **API Reference:** [rcwRegCreateRegistrant](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Registrants/rcwRegCreateRegistrant) **Example request body:** ```json { "firstName": "Luke", "lastName": "Skywalker", "email": "luke.skywalker@jedi.org", "externalId": "crm-lead-123", "source": "website-campaign-123" } ``` **JavaScript example** (from `registering.js`): ```javascript var sessionId = '' var endpoint = '/webinar/registration/v1/sessions/' + sessionId + '/registrants' platform.post(endpoint, { firstName: 'Luke', lastName: 'Skywalker', email: 'luke.skywalker@jedi.org', externalId: 'crm-lead-123', source: 'website-campaign-123' }) ``` ### Useful registrant fields for marketing integrations | Field | Purpose | |---|---| | `firstName`, `lastName`, `email` | Required attendee info | | `source` | Campaign/channel (e.g. `customer-invite`, `search-ad`) | | `externalId` | CRM lead/contact/opportunity ID for post-webinar follow-up | | `joinUri` | Custom join page URL (overrides RingCentral-hosted URL) | | `cancellationUri` | Custom cancellation page URL | The response includes a unique **`joinUri`** per registrant for tracking attendance. ### List registrants **Endpoint:** `GET /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants?perPage=100&includeQuestionnaire=false` **Operation:** `rcwRegListRegistrants` **Header:** `accept: application/json` --- ## 6. Registration session preferences (optional) Manage registration behavior per session via **`rcwRegUpdateSession`** ([update registration session](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Registration-Management/rcwRegUpdateSession)): | Preference | Purpose | |---|---| | `registrationStatus` | Turn registration on/off for the session | | `registrantCount` | Read how many people have registered | | `autoCloseLimit` | Close registration after N registrants | | `suppressEmails` | Suppress RingCentral registration emails (use your own) | | `preventMultipleDeviceJoins` | Block joining from multiple devices | Use `suppressEmails: true` when you run your own confirmation/reminder emails from a CRM or marketing tool. --- ## 7. Typical marketing webinar workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Create REST API app + JWT] --> B["POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars
(registrationEnabled: true)"] B --> C["POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions"] C --> D[Optional: rcwRegUpdateSession preferences] D --> E[Capture sign-ups on your landing page] E --> F["POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants"] F --> G[Send each attendee their unique joinUri] G --> H[Track attendance via participantId after join] ``` 1. Create webinar with `registrationEnabled: true` 2. Schedule a session 3. Optionally configure registration preferences (`suppressEmails`, `autoCloseLimit`, etc.) 4. Capture sign-ups on your own page or CRM form 5. Call **Create Registrant** for each attendee 6. Send each person their unique `joinUri` 7. After the event, compare registrant `id` vs `participantId` to see who attended --- ## 8. Related APIs (not for attendee registration) **Invite hosts/cohosts/panelists** (not attendees): **Endpoint:** `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions/{sessionId}/invitees` ```json { "addedInvitees": [ { "firstName": "Leia", "lastName": "Organa", "email": "leia.organa@newrepublic.gov", "role": "Cohost", "sendInvite": true } ] } ``` Roles include `Cohost` and `Panelist`. This is separate from the registrant flow. --- ## 9. Webhooks for registration tracking Subscribe via [rcwN11sCreateSubscription](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/Webinar-Subscriptions/rcwN11sCreateSubscription) with `eventFilters` such as: | Event filter | Fires when | |---|---| | `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/registrants` | A registrant is created or modified | | `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/state` | Registration settings change | | `/webinar/runtime/v1/company/sessions/state` | Webinar starts or ends | Refine with query params, e.g.: `/webinar/registration/v1/company/sessions/registrants?webinarId={webinarId}` Supported filter parameters: `webinarId`, `hostUserId`. --- ## Summary | Step | Endpoint | Key for marketing webinars | |---|---|---| | Create webinar | `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars` | Set `settings.registrationEnabled: true` | | Schedule session | `POST /webinar/configuration/v1/webinars/{webinarId}/sessions` | Get `sessionId` | | Add attendee | `POST /webinar/registration/v1/sessions/{sessionId}/registrants` | Use `source`, `externalId`; send unique `joinUri` | | Manage registration | `rcwRegUpdateSession` | `suppressEmails`, `autoCloseLimit`, etc. | The Webinar API is currently in **beta**. For full request/response schemas, use the [Webinar API Reference](https://developers.ringcentral.com/api-reference/webinar) and the [Webinar Quick Start](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/webinar/quick-start).

scores.raw_reasoning: Answer A correctly describes the two-step create-webinar-then-session flow, covers all key settings (registrationEnabled, recordingEnabled, autoRecord, qnaEnabled, pollsEnabled, password, attendeeAuthentication), explains marketing vs internal registration behavior with unique join URLs, documents the create-registrant endpoint, and explicitly states EditWebinars and ReadWebinars permissions.
scores.mintlify_reasoning: Answer B accurately covers the webinar/session workflow, key settings, marketing registration behavior, unique join URLs, and the create-registrant endpoint, but omits the required EditWebinars and ReadWebinars permissions called out in the ground truth.

results.question: What CRM platforms does RingCentral have native phone integration support for, and what does the rc-unified-crm-extension framework provide?
results.ground_truth: RingCentral has native phone integrations for: HubSpot (hubspot-embeddable-ringcentral-phone), Pipedrive (pipedrive-embeddable-ringcentral-phone), and Redtail (redtail-embeddable-ringcentral-phone). The rc-unified-crm-extension (App Connect) is a developer SDK/framework for building custom CRM adapters — it provides standardized call logging, contact lookup, and click-to-dial capabilities. Developers use it to connect any CRM to the RingCentral embeddable phone widget.

raw.answer:
## Native phone integrations

RingCentral ships **three** ready-made native phone integrations — Chrome extensions that embed the RingCentral phone widget into CRM UIs:

| CRM | Repository |
|-----|------------|
| **HubSpot** | `hubspot-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Pipedrive** | `pipedrive-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Redtail** | `redtail-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |

These extensions provide click-to-call, inbound caller pop-ups, and call log/voicemail/SMS sync back to the CRM.

---

## `rc-unified-crm-extension` (App Connect)

**App Connect** (`rc-unified-crm-extension`) is a **developer SDK/framework** for building custom CRM adapters. It standardizes the integration surface so developers can connect any CRM to the RingCentral embeddable phone widget.

Core capabilities:

- **Call logging** — standardized sync of calls and messages to CRM records  
- **Contact lookup** — find contacts by phone or name during calls  
- **Click-to-dial** — initiate calls from CRM contact pages  

Under the hood, the framework also provides a modular Express API, a CRM connector registry, OAuth/API-key authentication, contact management, analytics, and an MCP server for AI-assisted CRM workflows.

App Connect itself ships as a unified Chrome/Edge extension with built-in connectors for additional platforms (e.g., Bullhorn, Clio, Insightly, NetSuite, Freshdesk, GoHighLevel), but the three embeddable repos above are the dedicated per-CRM native phone widgets called out in the developer docs.

mintlify.answer:
## Native phone integration CRM platforms

Per the [Specialized SDKs](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/sdks#specialized-sdks) documentation, RingCentral ships **three ready-made native phone integrations** built on the App Connect framework (`rc-unified-crm-extension`):

| CRM platform | Package name |
|---|---|
| **HubSpot** | `hubspot-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Pipedrive** | `pipedrive-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |
| **Redtail** | `redtail-embeddable-ringcentral-phone` |

These are embeddable RingCentral phone widgets that run inside each CRM’s UI.

---

## What `rc-unified-crm-extension` provides

**Package name:** `rc-unified-crm-extension`  
**Product name:** App Connect  
**Documentation:** [https://ringcentral.github.io/rc-unified-crm-extension/](https://ringcentral.github.io/rc-unified-crm-extension/)

It is a **developer SDK/framework for building CRM integrations**. It standardizes three core telephony–CRM capabilities:

1. **Call logging** — sync call activity to the CRM  
2. **Contact lookup** — find CRM contacts (e.g., by phone number on inbound calls)  
3. **Click-to-dial** — place calls from CRM records

RingCentral uses this framework as the foundation for the three native embeddable phone integrations above, and developers can use it to build integrations for other CRMs.

---

## Related: broader App Connect CRM support (MCP)

The same App Connect product also powers an MCP server for AI-assisted CRM workflows. That layer supports **additional CRM platforms** beyond the three native phone integrations — including **Salesforce**, **HubSpot**, **Zoho**, and others (full list via the `getPublicConnectors` tool).

| Detail | Value |
|---|---|
| **MCP endpoint** | `https://unified-crm-extension.labs.ringcentral.com/mcp` |
| **Transport** | SSE over HTTPS |
| **Auth** | OAuth 2.0 / SSO (RingCentral) + CRM OAuth connection |

MCP tools include `findContactByPhone`, `findContactByName`, `createContact`, `createCallLog`, and `rcGetCallLogs`.

---

**Summary:** Native **embeddable phone** integrations exist for **HubSpot**, **Pipedrive**, and **Redtail**. The `rc-unified-crm-extension` framework provides standardized **call logging**, **contact lookup**, and **click-to-dial** so you can build CRM telephony integrations (including those three shipped products). Broader CRM connector support via App Connect/MCP covers additional platforms like Salesforce and Zoho, but those are not listed as the three ready-made native phone embeddables.

scores.raw_reasoning: Correctly lists HubSpot, Pipedrive, and Redtail with the right repo names and accurately describes rc-unified-crm-extension/App Connect as a developer SDK providing call logging, contact lookup, and click-to-dial to connect any CRM to the embeddable phone widget.
scores.mintlify_reasoning: Correctly identifies all three native CRM integrations and fully explains rc-unified-crm-extension/App Connect with standardized call logging, contact lookup, and click-to-dial for building custom CRM telephony adapters.

results.question: How long does a RingCentral OAuth access token last, and how do I get a new one without forcing the user to log in again? What happens when the refresh token also expires?
results.ground_truth: Access tokens last approximately 3600 seconds (1 hour). Use the refresh_token grant type (grant_type=refresh_token) to get a new access token silently before or after expiry — no user interaction needed. Refresh tokens for Auth Code flow last 7 days. If the refresh token expires, the user must re-authenticate from scratch (full OAuth flow). For JWT (server-to-server) flows there is no refresh token — simply request a new access token using the JWT grant at any time. Always cache and reuse tokens; calling the token endpoint too frequently triggers the Auth rate limit (5 requests/user/minute).

raw.answer:
## Access token lifetime

RingCentral OAuth **access tokens last about 1 hour** (~3600 seconds). The token response includes `expires_in` — store that value with the issue timestamp and treat it as the source of truth for when to refresh.

## Getting a new access token without re-login

For **Authorization Code** (or **Auth Code + PKCE**) apps, use the **refresh token flow**. No user interaction is required.

1. **Enable refresh tokens** in your app’s Developer Console settings (“Issue refresh tokens”). They are **not issued by default**.

2. **Store both tokens** after the initial authorization, along with issue time and the `expires_in` / `refresh_token_expires_in` values from the response.

3. **Refresh proactively** before the access token expires (don’t wait for HTTP 401):

```http
POST /restapi/oauth/token HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Authorization: Basic {base64(client_id:client_secret)}

grant_type=refresh_token&refresh_token={your_refresh_token}
```

For **client-side web apps**, omit the `Authorization` header and pass `client_id` in the body instead.

Each refresh typically returns a **new access token and a new refresh token**. Refresh tokens are **single-use** — always persist the latest pair. The docs recommend refreshing in a single dedicated service (especially in multithreaded apps) to avoid race conditions.

**Important:** Cache and reuse access tokens. The token endpoint is rate-limited under the **Auth** usage plan (**5 requests/user/minute** by default). Refreshing on every API call will hit that limit quickly.

## When the refresh token expires

Refresh tokens for the Auth Code flow last **7 days**. If the refresh token expires (or is invalid), **the user must re-authenticate from scratch** through the full OAuth authorization flow — there is no silent recovery.

## JWT flows (server-to-server)

If you’re using **JWT auth** instead of Auth Code, there is **no refresh token**. JWT credentials themselves don’t expire (unless you configure them to), but access tokens still do. When one expires, simply request a new access token with the JWT grant (`grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer`) — again, no user login required.

mintlify.answer:
## Access token lifetime

RingCentral OAuth access tokens last **1 hour** (~**3600 seconds**). The token response includes an `expires_in` field; store that value with the token and its issue time rather than hard-coding the TTL.

| Token | Time to live |
|-------|--------------|
| Access token | **1 hour** |
| Refresh token | **7 days** |

Source: [Refresh tokens](https://ringcentral.mintlify.app/authentication/refresh-tokens)

---

## Getting a new access token without re-login

Use the **refresh token flow**. This works for apps that obtained tokens via the **Authorization Code** or **Authorization Code + PKCE** flows.

### Prerequisites

1. Enable **"Issue refresh tokens"** in your app settings in the Developer Console. Refresh tokens are **not issued by default**.
2. Store both the access token and refresh token after the initial OAuth exchange.

### Refresh request

**Endpoint:** `POST https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/oauth/token`

**Headers:**

| Header | Value |
|--------|-------|
| `Content-Type` | `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` |
| `Authorization` | `Basic ` + base64(`Client ID:Client Secret`) — required for server-side apps; **omit** for "Client-side web app" type |

**POST body parameters:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| `grant_type` | `refresh_token` |
| `refresh_token` | The refresh token to exchange |
| `client_id` | Required only for "Client-side web app" type |

**Example:**

```http
POST /restapi/oauth/token HTTP/1.1
Accept: application/json
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

refresh_token=BCMDFUMDRKV1MwMXx5d5dwzLFL4ec6U1A0XMsUv935527jghj48
   &grant_type=refresh_token&client_id=adsadsadsadadsad
```

On success you get a new access token (valid for another hour) and usually a **new refresh token**. No user login is required.

### Best practices

- **Refresh proactively** — check `expires_in` (and `refresh_token_expires_in`) before each API call; do not wait for `HTTP 401`.
- **Run a background job at least once per day** to refresh stored tokens so sessions stay alive.
- **Persist the new refresh token** after each refresh — refresh tokens are **single-use**.
- **Avoid race conditions** in multi-threaded apps; use a dedicated token-refresh service.

### Using an SDK

Official SDK packages include:

| Language | Package | Install |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Python | `ringcentral` | `pip3 install ringcentral` |
| JavaScript/Node | `@ringcentral/sdk` | `npm install @ringcentral/sdk` |
| PHP | `ringcentral/ringcentral-php` | `composer require ringcentral/ringcentral-php` |
| Ruby | `ringcentral_sdk` | `gem install ringcentral_sdk` |
| Java | `com.ringcentral:ringcentral` | Maven dependency |
| Go | `github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-go` | `go get github.com/ringcentral/ringcentral-go` |
| .NET/C# | `RingCentral.Net` | `dotnet add package RingCentral.Net` |

SDKs expose a refresh helper — e.g. **`platform.refresh()`** in Ruby/Node — which you can call on a timer (daily or every 6 days) to keep the refresh token from expiring.

---

## Important refresh-token behavior

- Refresh tokens are **single-use**; a new one is usually issued on each refresh.
- After a refresh, the **old access token is immediately invalidated** (`HTTP 401` if reused).
- If you **use** the new access token, the old refresh token becomes invalid in ~**10 seconds**.
- If you **don't use** the new access token, the old refresh token stays valid for up to **60 minutes** (same access token returned on repeat refresh requests).

---

## When the refresh token also expires

Refresh tokens last **7 days**. If you don't refresh within that window, the refresh token expires too.

**What happens:**

1. A refresh request to `/restapi/oauth/token` **fails** (the docs say not to retry failed token requests; `HTTP 400` is listed for token endpoint failures).
2. API calls with the expired access token return **`HTTP 401 Unauthorized`**.
3. The user **must re-authenticate from scratch** — redirect them through the OAuth authorization flow again:

   `https://platform.ringcentral.com/restapi/oauth/authorize?response_type=code&client_id=<client_id>&redirect_uri=<redirect_uri>`

There is no silent recovery once the refresh token is expired. The docs are explicit: *"If the refresh token expires, the user must re-authenticate from scratch."*

Other things that invalidate tokens (same outcome — re-auth required):

- User revokes the app session
- User changes password
- Admin **Absolute Session Timeout** policy
- App hits the parallel session limit (usually **5** active sessions per user)

---

## Alternative: JWT flow (no refresh tokens)

For **server-side automation** with a single service account (no per-user login), use the **JWT auth flow** instead. JWT flows **do not issue refresh tokens**; you request a new access token by POSTing to the same token endpoint with:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| `grant_type` | `urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer` |
| `assertion` | Your JWT credential |

This avoids user re-login, but is **not recommended** when multiple users need individual authentication — use Auth Code + PKCE for that.

---

**Bottom line:** Access tokens expire every hour; use `grant_type=refresh_token` to silently renew them for up to 7 days. Refresh at least daily to keep sessions alive indefinitely. Once the 7-day refresh token expires, the user must go through the full OAuth login flow again.

scores.raw_reasoning: Covers all key facts—3600s access token TTL, grant_type=refresh_token silent renewal, 7-day refresh token, full re-auth when it expires, JWT flow without refresh tokens, and the 5 requests/user/minute rate limit—with no significant errors.
scores.mintlify_reasoning: Accurately covers access token TTL, refresh_token grant, 7-day refresh lifetime, re-authentication requirement, and JWT flow, but omits the Auth rate limit of 5 requests/user/minute.
